content:
# Disagreement Log — Joshua 10
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

This log records all verses in Joshua Chapter 10 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 10:1 — "had devoted it to destruction" vs. "put it under the ban" for hechrim

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "had-devoted-it-to-destruction" | Literal Hiphil rendering of cherem root |
| LOGOS | "had devoted it to destruction" | Same rendering, natural phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "put it under the ban" | Captures the ritual/legal dimension of cherem |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "had devoted it to destruction" (EMET, LOGOS). The phrase "devoted to destruction" has been the SOPHIA standard for cherem in prior chapters (cf. Joshua 2:10). While PNEUMA's "put under the ban" captures an important nuance (the sacral-legal character of cherem), "devoted to destruction" more transparently conveys the semantic content. The term is used repeatedly in vv. 28, 35, 37, 39, and 40, requiring consistency.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. This decision applies to all cherem occurrences throughout the chapter (vv. 1, 28, 35, 37, 39, 40).

---

## 10:2 — "they feared greatly" vs. "the fear was enormous"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and-they-feared greatly" | Preserves plural verb and adverb directly |
| LOGOS | "they feared greatly" | Same, with natural English phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "the fear was enormous" | Impersonal rendering to capture atmospheric sense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "they feared greatly" (EMET, LOGOS). The MT reads vayyir'u me'od with a third-person plural verb. PNEUMA's impersonal construction changes the grammatical subject and adds "enormous" (the Hebrew me'od means "greatly/very," not "enormous"). The plural subject preserves the ambiguity the Hebrew allows — the fear was shared.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 10:5 — "camps" vs. "armies" for machaneihem

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "their-camps" | Literal rendering of machaneihem |
| LOGOS | "their camps" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "their armies" | Dynamic rendering; the camps contain the armies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "their camps" (EMET, LOGOS). The Hebrew machaneh means "camp/encampment," and the plural refers to the assembled military encampments. PNEUMA's "armies" interprets the metonymy rather than translating the term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Minor lexical divergence.

---

## 10:6 — "Do not let your hands drop from your servants" vs. "Do not abandon your servants"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do-not-let-drop your-hands from-your-servants" | Preserves Hebrew idiom literally |
| LOGOS | "Do not let your hand drop from your servants" | Same idiom, natural English (singular "hand") |
| PNEUMA | "Do not abandon your servants" | Interprets the idiom to its meaning |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Do not let your hands drop from your servants" (EMET), correcting LOGOS's singular "hand" to the MT plural yadekha. Note: LOGOS reads "hand" (singular) where the MT has the plural form. The idiom is vivid and comprehensible; PNEUMA's interpretive rendering loses the concrete image. SOPHIA preserves Hebrew idioms where they are intelligible in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. Three-way divergence on idiom treatment.

---

## 10:6 — "the inhabitants of the hill country" vs. "who dwell in the hill country"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the]-inhabitants of-the-hill-country" | Participial noun rendering |
| LOGOS | "who dwell in the hill country" | Relative clause rendering |
| PNEUMA | "from the hill country" | Compressed; omits the dwelling language |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a combined rendering: "the inhabitants of the hill country" — preserving the participial construction (EMET) with natural phrasing. PNEUMA's compression loses the explicit identification of these kings as hill-country dwellers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. Minor structural divergence.

---

## 10:7 — "mighty men of the valor" vs. "best warriors"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the]-mighty-men of-the-valor" | Literal rendering of gibborei hachayil |
| LOGOS | "the mighty men of valor" | Same, natural phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "his best warriors" | Dynamic rendering of the title |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the mighty men of valor" (LOGOS). The phrase gibborei hachayil is a fixed military title in Hebrew. PNEUMA's "best warriors" captures the sense but loses the technical designation. EMET and LOGOS agree on the formal rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 10:10 — "threw them into confusion" vs. "threw them into panic" for wayhummem

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "confused-them" | Root h-m-m rendered as confusion |
| LOGOS | "threw them into confusion" | Same root, expanded phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "threw them into panic" | Interprets the effect as panic rather than confusion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "threw them into confusion" (LOGOS). The root h-m-m denotes confusion, disorientation, divine disruption of coherent action (cf. Exodus 14:24, 1 Samuel 7:10). "Panic" emphasizes the emotional response; "confusion" is closer to the semantic core of the root, which is about the loss of ordered capacity. EMET's compressed form is too terse for a consensus text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor semantic divergence.

---

## 10:10 — "striking" vs. "slaughter" for makkah

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[with]-a-striking great" | Preserves cognate accusative construction |
| LOGOS | "with a great striking" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "with a great slaughter" | Interprets makkah as slaughter |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with a great striking" (EMET, LOGOS). The Hebrew uses the cognate accusative (vayakkem makkah gedolah — "struck them a great striking"), and preserving "striking" maintains the etymological link to the verb nakah. "Slaughter" imports an interpretation. This cognate construction recurs in v. 20.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Recurring pattern in chapter.

---

## 10:11 — Hailstone sequence: two-stage vs. combined

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "stones great from the-heavens...stones of-the-hail" | Preserves MT's two-stage description |
| LOGOS | "great stones from the heavens...hailstones" | Same two-stage structure |
| PNEUMA | "enormous hailstones...from the sky" | Combines into a single description |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted the two-stage structure (EMET, LOGOS): "great stones...from the heavens" followed by "stones of the hail." The MT deliberately introduces "great stones" first and then identifies them as hailstones in the comparison clause. This narrative technique — presenting the phenomenon and then interpreting it — is a feature of the Hebrew text that PNEUMA's compression eliminates. Additionally, PNEUMA's "enormous" goes beyond the Hebrew gedolot ("great").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Significant narrative structure divergence.

---

## 10:12 — "be still" vs. "stand still" for dom

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "be-still" | Root d-m-m means "be silent, cease" |
| LOGOS | "be still" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "stand still" | Interprets as cessation of motion specifically |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "be still" (EMET, LOGOS). The Hebrew imperative dom derives from the root d-m-m, meaning "be silent, be still, cease." This is semantically distinct from the verb 'amad ("stand") used in v. 13 to describe the sun's response. Joshua commands the sun to "be still/cease" (dom), and the text reports that it "stood" ('amad). PNEUMA's "stand still" collapses this distinction by importing the vocabulary of v. 13 into v. 12. The LXX similarly interprets with steto ("let it stand"), but the MT preserves a meaningful verbal distinction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Theologically and lexically significant — the distinction between commanding silence/cessation and the resulting standing is a deliberate textual feature.

---

## 10:12 — "in the sight of Israel" vs. "in the sight of Israel" — poetry formatting

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | Prose format, continuous | Renders as narrative prose |
| LOGOS | Set-off poetry with line breaks | Marks register shift to poetry |
| PNEUMA | Blockquote poetry with emphasis | Full poetic formatting with ">" markers |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted set-off poetry format (LOGOS, supported by PNEUMA). The sun/moon address is a quotation from the Book of the Upright, a poetic collection. The register shift from narrative prose to direct imperative address of celestial bodies warrants visual distinction. SOPHIA uses line breaks without blockquote markers, following LOGOS's cleaner formatting.

**Status:** Resolved as LOGOS/PNEUMA formatting. Not a translation divergence per se, but a presentation decision.

---

## 10:13 — "Book of the Upright" vs. "Book of Jashar"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Book of-the-Upright" | Translates yashar ("upright, straight") |
| LOGOS | "Book of the Upright" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Book of Jashar" | Transliterates the proper name |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Book of the Upright" (EMET, LOGOS). The Hebrew Sefer HaYashar contains the word yashar, which has a clear and theologically significant meaning: "upright, straight, just." Transliterating as "Jashar" obscures this meaning without adding clarity. The book's title — "The Book of the Upright" — is itself a theological statement about the character of the heroes it celebrates. PNEUMA follows modern convention, but SOPHIA's mandate is to render Hebrew meanings rather than merely transliterate them.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Lexically significant.

---

## 10:13 — "did not hasten to go down" vs. "was in no hurry to set"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "did-not-hasten to-go-in" | Literal rendering of lo 'ats lavo' |
| LOGOS | "did not hasten to go down" | Preserves anthropomorphism with "go down" for sunset |
| PNEUMA | "was in no hurry to set" | Dynamic, idiomatic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "did not hasten to go down" (LOGOS). The Hebrew lo 'ats lavo' literally reads "did not hurry to go/come." LOGOS's "go down" appropriately interprets lavo' in context (the sun going down), which is standard. EMET's "go-in" is overly literal (lavo' can mean "come, go, enter"). PNEUMA's "was in no hurry to set" is an attractive rendering but replaces the Hebrew's anthropomorphism with a more colloquial English idiom; "did not hasten" preserves the formal register.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Three-way divergence on degree of interpretation.

---

## 10:14 — "when YHWH listened" vs. "for YHWH to listen" — infinitive clause

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for-hearing YHWH [the]-voice of-a-man" | Preserves infinitive construct with lamed |
| LOGOS | "when YHWH listened to the voice of a man" | Renders as temporal clause |
| PNEUMA | "a day when YHWH listened to the voice of a man" | Expanded temporal clause |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "when YHWH listened to the voice of a man" (LOGOS, PNEUMA). The Hebrew lishmo'a YHWH b'qol 'ish uses the infinitive construct with lamed, which can express purpose, cause, or temporal circumstance. In context, the temporal/causal reading is most natural: the day was unique because of the circumstance that YHWH listened to a human voice. EMET's rendering is grammatically faithful but opaque in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Grammatical divergence; EMET too literal for clarity.

---

## 10:19 — "strike their rear" vs. "attack their rear" vs. "cut down their rear guard"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you-shall-strike-their-rear" | Literal rendering of zanav (to tail, attack the rear) |
| LOGOS | "attack their rear" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "cut down their rear guard" | Dynamic; adds "rear guard" as military term |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "strike their rear" (EMET, modified). The Hebrew verb zanav means "to attack the tail/rear." SOPHIA uses "strike" for consistency with the dominant verb nakah throughout the chapter. LOGOS's "attack" is acceptable but less specific. PNEUMA's "cut down their rear guard" adds interpretive material ("rear guard" is not in the text, and "cut down" is not the verb used).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of modified EMET. Minor divergence.

---

## 10:20 — "striking" vs. "slaughter" for makkah (second occurrence)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[with]-a-striking very great" | Cognate accusative preserved |
| LOGOS | "with a very great striking" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "with a very great slaughter" | Interprets as slaughter |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with a very great striking" (EMET, LOGOS), consistent with the decision at v. 10.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Consistent with prior decision.

---

## 10:21 — "No man sharpened his tongue" vs. "No one dared to say a word"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not sharpened a-man against-[the]-sons of-Israel his-tongue" | Hyper-literal word order |
| LOGOS | "No man sharpened his tongue against the sons of Israel" | Preserves idiom with natural word order |
| PNEUMA | "No one dared to say a word against the Sons of Israel" | Interprets the idiom |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "No man sharpened his tongue against the sons of Israel" (LOGOS). The Hebrew idiom lo charats...leshono (cf. Exodus 11:7) is vivid and distinctive — it means to whet the tongue as one whets a blade, i.e., to speak sharply or hostilely. LOGOS preserves the idiom with readable word order. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the metaphorical power. EMET's word order is too disruptive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Significant idiom-preservation decision.

---

## 10:24 — "Draw near" vs. "Come forward" vs. "Come-near"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Come-near" | Literal qirvu |
| LOGOS | "Draw near" | Formal English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Come forward" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Draw near" (LOGOS). "Draw near" has a more solemn, ceremonial register appropriate to the ritual act of subjugation being commanded. "Come forward" is adequate but lacks gravity. "Come-near" is acceptable but the hyphenation is a feature of EMET's method.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor register divergence.

---

## 10:25 — "do not be dismayed" vs. "do not lose heart"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do-not-be-dismayed" | Literal rendering of al techatu |
| LOGOS | "do not be dismayed" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "do not lose heart" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "do not be dismayed" (EMET, LOGOS). The Hebrew al techatu (from chatat) means "do not be shattered/broken/dismayed." "Dismayed" is the standard formal equivalent and preserves the force of the original. "Lose heart" shifts from an external condition (being shattered) to an internal decision.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 10:26 — "struck them" vs. "struck them down" for vayakkem

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "struck-them" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "struck them" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "struck them down" | Adds "down" for clarity of lethal force |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "struck them" (EMET, LOGOS). The verse continues with "and put them to death," making PNEUMA's added "down" redundant. The Hebrew nakah does not inherently include "down."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Minor addition by PNEUMA.

---

## 10:27 — "unto this very day" — rendering of 'ad-'etsem hayom hazeh

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unto this the-day the-very" | Hyper-literal with Hebrew demonstrative pattern |
| LOGOS | "unto this very day" | Natural English with intensifier preserved |
| PNEUMA | "they are there to this day" | Paraphrastic expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "unto this very day" (LOGOS). The Hebrew 'etsem ("bone, substance, very") intensifies the demonstrative. LOGOS captures this with "very." EMET's rendering is opaque; PNEUMA's paraphrase adds "they are there" which is implicit but not stated.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Three-way divergence on idiom treatment.

---

## 10:28 — "edge of the sword" vs. "with the sword" for l'phi-cherev

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "by-[the]-mouth of-[the]-sword" | Preserves Hebrew metaphor (pi = mouth) |
| LOGOS | "with the edge of the sword" | Standard English equivalent preserving the image |
| PNEUMA | "with the sword" | Simplified |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with the edge of the sword" (LOGOS). The Hebrew l'phi-cherev literally means "to the mouth of the sword," an image of the sword devouring. "Edge" is the standard English equivalent that preserves the idea of the sword's cutting part (its "mouth"). EMET's "mouth of the sword" is more literal but may confuse readers; PNEUMA's plain "with the sword" loses the distinctive Hebrew image. This rendering applies to all occurrences in vv. 28, 30, 32, 35, 37, 39.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Recurring formula throughout vv. 28-39.

---

## 10:30 — Word order: "YHWH gave it also, and its king, into the hand of Israel"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And-gave YHWH also it in-[the]-hand of-Israel, and-its-king" | Follows Hebrew word order |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH also gave it into the hand of Israel, and its king" | English word order |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH gave it too, along with its king, into Israel's hand" | Dynamic restructuring |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "And YHWH gave it also, and its king, into the hand of Israel" — a rendering that follows LOGOS's basic structure but preserves the Hebrew placement of "and its king" as a secondary object (following EMET's observation of the MT word order). PNEUMA's "Israel's hand" shortens the construct chain unnecessarily.

**Status:** Resolved as a SOPHIA synthesis. Three-way divergence on word order.

---

## 10:33 — "went up" vs. "came up" for 'alah

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "went-up" | Literal 'alah |
| LOGOS | "came up" | English perspective (approaching Lachish) |
| PNEUMA | "came up" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "went up" (EMET). The Hebrew 'alah means "to go up, ascend," and from the narrator's perspective, Horam goes up from Gezer toward Lachish. "Came up" implies the narrator's viewpoint is at Lachish, which is possible but less neutral. EMET's rendering is more faithful to the verb's basic meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. Minor perspective divergence.

---

## 10:37 — "all its cities" vs. "all its towns"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "all its-cities" | Literal 'areha |
| LOGOS | "all its cities" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "all its towns" | Dynamic; "towns" for dependent settlements |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "all its cities" (EMET, LOGOS). The Hebrew 'ir (plural 'arim) is rendered "city" consistently throughout SOPHIA. While PNEUMA's point that dependent settlements may be smaller is valid, the Hebrew does not make this distinction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Consistent lexical choice.

---

## 10:40 — "all that breathed" vs. "everything that breathed" for kol hanneshamah

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "all the-breathing" | Hyper-literal participial |
| LOGOS | "all that breathed" | Natural English, preserves neshamah |
| PNEUMA | "everything that breathed" | Same sense, different pronoun |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "all that breathed" (LOGOS). EMET's "all the-breathing" is awkward; LOGOS and PNEUMA are nearly identical. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's "all" rather than PNEUMA's "everything" for conciseness and closer alignment with kol.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 10:42 — "at one time" vs. "in a single campaign" for b'pa'am 'achath

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "at-time one" | Literal rendering |
| LOGOS | "at one time" | Natural English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "in a single campaign" | Interpretive; reads military strategy |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "at one time" (LOGOS). The Hebrew b'pa'am 'achath means "at one time, at once, all together." It emphasizes temporal compression — everything happened in one sweep. PNEUMA's "in a single campaign" adds a military-strategic interpretation that the Hebrew does not require. The point is simultaneity and divine speed, not campaign planning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Interpretive divergence.

---

## Summary of Resolutions

| Resolution | Count |
|---|---|
| EMET/LOGOS (consensus against PNEUMA) | 14 |
| LOGOS alone | 5 |
| EMET alone | 2 |
| LOGOS/PNEUMA (consensus against EMET) | 1 |
| SOPHIA synthesis | 1 |

The pattern in this chapter is clear: EMET and LOGOS agreed closely on the vast majority of renderings, while PNEUMA diverged through dynamic equivalence, idiom interpretation, and occasional vocabulary additions. SOPHIA's consensus text follows the EMET/LOGOS center in most cases, drawing on PNEUMA primarily for formatting decisions (the poetry of vv. 12-13) and recognizing PNEUMA's interpretive notes as valuable commentary even where the renderings were not adopted.
